caveats: a: The occupation source covers utilities broadly and includes distribution and nuclear roles outside 221118.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio is high for an asset-intensive industry and may be distorted by the small residual population or vintage mismatch.
rho: No direct five-year adoption survey for 221118 was found.
rho: Implementation varies sharply across fuel cells, waste-derived sources, storage-like facilities, purchased-steam systems, and other residual technologies.
e: The frozen 32-firm estimate is margin-bridged and may count asset entities rather than operating platforms.
e: NAICS is a residual category whose establishments can have materially different technologies and ownership structures.
s5: Cross-industry intentions are not completed power-industry transactions.
s5: Power-project asset and affiliate transfers can look like firm sales but fail the lens's operating-platform requirement.
q: No public contract sample isolates automation pass-through.
q: Retention differs substantially between merchant, fixed-price PPA, cost-based, and behind-the-meter arrangements.
d5: Total electricity growth is not demand for the residual technology basket.
d5: EIA energy-source categories do not map cleanly to establishment NAICS and may include storage or fuels classified elsewhere.
o: The accountable entity may consolidate across many facilities, reducing local staffing without eliminating an operator.
o: Storage and behind-the-meter systems may shift responsibility to customers or aggregators more readily than combustion or process-based generators.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring wholesale electricity, capacity, renewable or environmental attribute, and ancillary-service revenue through long-term power purchase agreements, utility contracts, regulated or market-based rates, and organized spot or forward markets; revenue depends on dispatched or contracted output, availability, price, and compliance.
lens.excluded_activities: The specifically classified generation technologies, transmission and distribution utilities, equipment vendors, developers without operating assets, captive self-generation without external sales, municipal or internal departments, passive asset shells, tax-equity vehicles, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market operators of electric power generation facilities using technologies outside hydroelectric, fossil fuel, nuclear, solar, wind, geothermal, and biomass, selling recurring electricity, capacity, or ancillary services to utilities, organized markets, aggregators, or contracted commercial and industrial offtakers.

a.bridge: BLS describes power plant operators across generation technologies, not a wage-weighted task inventory for residual-code 221118. I map monitoring review, forecasting, dispatch support, preventive-maintenance analysis, work orders, market bids, settlements, contracts, regulatory reporting, environmental records, and administration into AI-exposed wages; the broader occupation may overstate physical control-room work for remote niche plants but understate commercial and compliance staff.
caveats: The occupation source covers utilities broadly and includes distribution and nuclear roles outside 221118.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio is high for an asset-intensive industry and may be distorted by the small residual population or vintage mismatch.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census from 10-15 eligible operators, separating control-room and field operations, maintenance, engineering, market operations, compliance, finance, and administration and documenting existing SCADA, remote monitoring, predictive maintenance, and workflow automation.
a.rationale: Modern plants already automate core controls, leaving additional AI opportunity in interpreting alarms and trends, predicting failures, planning maintenance, preparing market and compliance submissions, reconciling settlements, and administration. Continuous attention, field rounds, mechanical troubleshooting, emergency response, and safety-critical control remain human and physical.

d5.bridge: EIA forecasts total US electricity use and broad generation shares, not constant-price demand for 221118 technologies. I translate growing system load into a modestly positive central case, then widen the range because other sources were only 1 percent of the 2025-2027 mix and the residual basket can lose share to solar, wind, natural gas, or newer storage classifications even when total load grows.
caveats: Total electricity growth is not demand for the residual technology basket.
EIA energy-source categories do not map cleanly to establishment NAICS and may include storage or fuels classified elsewhere.
d5.change_evidence: EIA-860 and EIA-923 unit-level capacity, generation, retirements, planned additions, and ownership for facilities mapped to 221118, plus ISO queue and capacity-auction data, PPA renewals, fuel or input availability, and technology-specific levelized costs.
d5.rationale: Data centers, electrification, manufacturing, and general load growth support electricity demand, while capacity and grid services can reward dispatchable or specialized sources. Technology substitution, weak economics, expiring incentives or PPAs, feedstock constraints, and retirement of niche plants can outweigh market growth.

caveats: The frozen 32-firm estimate is margin-bridged and may count asset entities rather than operating platforms.
NAICS is a residual category whose establishments can have materially different technologies and ownership structures.
e.change_evidence: Map SUSB firms to EIA generator IDs, FERC sellers, state registrations, ultimate owners, PPAs, operating employees, and facility managers; verify normalized EBITDA, external revenue, asset and contract transferability, customer or offtaker concentration, technology, and whether operating capability sits in the entity.
e.rationale: Some firms are independent operators with contracted or market sales, but the residual code likely contains many project entities, captive facilities, public operators, single-asset shells, and businesses whose economics depend on tax, feedstock, host-site, or affiliate arrangements. Eligible firms must own transferable operating capabilities and external recurring revenue rather than only a financed asset.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 221118 ($736,094,252) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 221118 ($1,198,245,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3492 (margins.json[22]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Utility (General) EBITDA/Sales 34.92%); revenue/employee $1,258,661 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 221118); class <5: 54 firms, avg revenue/firm $754,204 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $263,368 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,426,600 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,291,769 -> IN $1-10M band; class 10-19: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,968,545 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,433,416 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,086,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,426,831 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,798,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,723,178 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $150,635,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $52,601,858 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 32

caveats: The accountable entity may consolidate across many facilities, reducing local staffing without eliminating an operator.
Storage and behind-the-meter systems may shift responsibility to customers or aggregators more readily than combustion or process-based generators.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on autonomous-plant approvals, third-party fleet control, owner self-operation, software-only participation models, outsourced O&M contracts, reliability events, insurance requirements, and regulator treatment of remote unattended operation by technology.
o.rationale: Electricity generation from physical facilities continues to require an accountable owner or operator for equipment, interconnection, dispatch, safety, maintenance, environmental obligations, market participation, and emergency response. Remote control and software can centralize work but do not eliminate legal and operational accountability for the current service basket.

caveats: No public contract sample isolates automation pass-through.
Retention differs substantially between merchant, fixed-price PPA, cost-based, and behind-the-meter arrangements.
q.change_evidence: A contract sample covering PPAs, tolling and host agreements, capacity and ancillary-service rules, cost pass-through, indexation, availability guarantees, open-book terms, renewable attributes, renewal dates, and project margins before and after operational automation.
q.rationale: Fixed-price or fixed-escalator PPAs, capacity commitments, and market clearing prices can let an operator retain maintenance, availability, forecasting, and back-office gains during the contract term. Cost-of-service treatment, shared-savings clauses, availability penalties, merchant competition, transparent bids, and PPA renewal repricing pass part of benefits to customers over five years.

caveats: No direct five-year adoption survey for 221118 was found.
Implementation varies sharply across fuel cells, waste-derived sources, storage-like facilities, purchased-steam systems, and other residual technologies.
rho.change_evidence: Audited pre/post deployments at comparable plants covering alarm triage, predictive maintenance, dispatch and bidding, EQR and environmental reporting, settlement exceptions, outage planning, and work orders, with labor hours, false alarms, forced outages, heat or conversion efficiency, compliance incidents, and availability.
rho.rationale: Decision support, predictive maintenance, reporting, settlement review, and administrative workflows are implementable within five years. Safety, cybersecurity, unreliable edge connectivity, vendor-locked controls, validation requirements, sparse failure data, technology-specific engineering, and operator accountability constrain autonomous execution.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sell or transfer intentions among US employer-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers of residual electric generators. I center near its 22 percent employer intention, allow for active power-asset ownership changes, and discount non-completion, affiliate restructurings, tax-equity changes, passive asset sales, and transfers lacking an operating platform.
caveats: Cross-industry intentions are not completed power-industry transactions.
Power-project asset and affiliate transfers can look like firm sales but fail the lens's operating-platform requirement.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of FERC change-in-status and section 203 filings, EIA ownership changes, PitchBook, IJGlobal, LevelTen, and brokered power-asset deals matched to a verified eligible-firm denominator and screened for control, operating-platform transfer, and completion.
s5.rationale: Long-lived contracted assets can be transferable, but permits, interconnection, PPAs, host or feedstock contracts, market authorization, liabilities, and change-of-control consents complicate completion. FERC reporting creates observable ownership evidence but does not itself provide a qualifying-transfer rate.

principal_driver: Contracted or market-priced recurring power revenue can retain gains from analytics, availability, maintenance, and reporting improvements.
principal_weakness: A tiny heterogeneous residual category with many asset entities makes eligible-firm identification and transferable operating-platform quality unusually uncertain.

caveats: The a proxy maps a broad occupation that includes distribution and nuclear roles into a 221118 wage share without a residual-code task census; the bridge is transparent, but the 0.22 base remains weakly anchored.

S2 and S3 establish reporting, sales, and filing structures but do not directly support the numeric rho, e, q, or o estimates; those values are honest bounded judgments rather than observed measurements.
The q interval remains wholly above the 0.50 factor cap, making C equal to 10 in every registered scenario despite the absence of a contract sample and acknowledged contract-model dispersion.
The d5 proxy extrapolates a short-horizon forecast for total U.S. electricity use and an EIA source basket that does not map cleanly to establishment NAICS into a five-year 221118 demand range.
The s5 proxy starts from cross-industry owner intentions rather than completed qualifying transfers of power operating platforms; the record discloses this limitation and supplies a broad range.

The o range of 0.96-0.999 is unusually tight for a heterogeneous residual technology basket without direct evidence on autonomous operation, outsourcing, aggregation, or technology-specific retirements.

The lens marks heterogeneous as false even though the record repeatedly describes the residual category as heterogeneous; this metadata inconsistency does not alter the explicit included and excluded activities or the base tier/action.

sources_audited.note: BLS supports the stated broad occupation duties, 2024 employment, projected decline, and control-room automation, but it does not provide a 221118-specific wage-weighted task share or direct evidence for the numeric a and rho estimates; that transformation is disclosed as a proxy or estimate.

FERC confirms that EQRs contain contractual terms and transaction information for short- and long-term cost-based and market-based power sales. It does not quantify AI implementation, eligible-firm prevalence, gain retention, or operator survival, so its support for rho, e, q, and o is contextual rather than numeric.

FERC confirms authorization for wholesale energy, capacity, and ancillary-service sales and the listed initial, change-in-status, triennial, tariff, succession, and cancellation filings. It does not quantify eligible platforms, transfer completion, or retention, so support for e, s5, and q is contextual.
The January 2026 EIA release reports 1% U.S. electricity-use growth in 2026, 3% in 2027, and a 1% share for other energy sources in 2025-2027, matching the stated broad demand proxy.
EIA reports other gases and other sources at about 0.2% of 2025 utility-scale generation and lists the stated heterogeneous component technologies and fuels.

Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners surveyed in fall 2024 planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, exactly the cross-industry intention input described by the proxy bridge.

sources.supports: Lists monitoring, control, equipment checks, adjustment, start and stop, field rounds, troubleshooting, and emergency-relevant duties; reports 46,600 jobs in 2024 and a projected 10 percent employment decline through 2034 as technology and modernized control rooms improve efficiency.
sources.title: Power Plant Operators, Distributors, and Dispatchers

sources.supports: Explains that EQR data include contractual terms and transaction information for short- and long-term market-based and cost-based power sales, grounding recurring revenue models, reporting workflows, and contract heterogeneity.
sources.title: Electric Quarterly Reports

sources.supports: Describes authorization for wholesale energy, capacity, and ancillary-service sellers and required initial, change-in-status, triennial, tariff, succession, and cancellation filings, grounding compliance and ownership-transfer considerations.
sources.title: Electric Market-Based Rates

sources.supports: Forecasts US electricity use growth of 1 percent in 2026 and 3 percent in 2027 and shows other energy sources at 1 percent of generation in 2025, 2026, and 2027, used as a broad demand proxy rather than a 221118 forecast.
sources.title: EIA forecasts strongest four-year growth in U.S. electricity demand since 2000, fueled by data centers

sources.supports: Reports other gases and other sources at about 0.2 percent of 2025 utility-scale generation and identifies other sources as including pumped storage, non-biogenic municipal waste, batteries, hydrogen, purchased steam, sulfur, tire-derived fuel, and miscellaneous sources, illustrating small scale and category heterogeneity.
sources.title: Electricity in the United States

sources.supports: Reports that 22 percent of employer-business owners surveyed in fall 2024 planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, used as a cross-industry transfer proxy.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

title: Other Electric Power Generation
